sections.title: Observing and Recalling Length
sections.prose_summary: The chapter opens by asking students to observe a classroom scene and identify what is being measured and with which tools. Students revisit prior knowledge from Grade 3 — that lengths are measured in metres — and check whether familiar classroom measurements (student height, arm length, door height, blackboard breadth) are more or less than a metre. This section is at the concrete–symbolic boundary: students are not yet measuring but activating body-scale intuition and checking everyday comparisons against the metre benchmark.

tasks.description: Look at the picture; put a tick mark if length, height, weight, depth, breadth, or temperature is being measured.
What is being used to measure height? What other tools can be used?
Recall Grade 3 learning; check and fill blanks — are the given classroom length statements correct or incorrect?

sections.title: Measuring with Metre Ropes
sections.prose_summary: Students re-make metre, half-metre, and quarter-metre ropes and physically walk, jump, and crawl along 1 m, 5 m, and 10 m lines to build body-scale intuition of metric distances. A long-jump competition challenges them to estimate then measure jump distances using combinations of ropes, filling a table of estimated versus actual measurements. Finally, students estimate and measure the length and breadth of their classroom. This section is firmly concrete: the rope is the manipulative model, and the focus is on developing embodied metric sense before any formal unit conversion.

tasks.description: Draw 1 m, 5 m, and 10 m lines on the floor; walk, jump, and crawl on the lines.
Long jump competition: estimate then measure each student's jump distance using rope combinations; fill the table.
Estimate how long and broad the classroom is; measure and check.

sections.title: Estimating Lengths of Animals and Objects
sections.prose_summary: Students examine pictures showing the lengths of a blue whale (~30 m), a crocodile (~6 m), an ostrich (~3 m), and a cricket bat (~1 m), then answer reasoning questions by comparing and combining these reference lengths. This pictorial section develops proportional thinking and the ability to use a known benchmark to reason about unknown quantities without direct measurement.

tasks.description: Answer 5 questions: bus length, bats per whale, buses per two whales, stacked ostriches, crocodiles per whale — using pictured reference lengths.

sections.title: Introducing Centimetre
sections.prose_summary: The need for a smaller unit is motivated by everyday objects (eraser, rice grain) that cannot be measured meaningfully in metres. Students observe a metre scale divided into 100 equal parts, learning that each part is one centimetre (cm) and that 1 m = 100 cm. A measuring tape is compared with the metre rope to highlight that the tape is graduated in centimetres. Students then use the tape to track a plant's weekly height. This section sits at the pictorial-to-symbolic transition: the scale and tape are the concrete models, and the key relationship 1 m = 100 cm is established.

tasks.description: Compare the metre rope with a tailor's measuring tape; observe the marks and discuss how they help measure clearly.
Observe the 1 cm red bar repeated 10 times to make 10 cm; fill: 1/2 m = ___ cm, 1/4 m = ___ cm.

sections.title: Measuring with Centimetre Scale
sections.prose_summary: A rich sequence of tasks consolidates centimetre measurement using a ruler or tape. Students measure drawn objects, estimate then verify small lengths (fingernail, ant, rajma seed), race toy cars and measure travel distances, navigate a treasure-hunt map counting 1 cm tiles, trace their hand and measure its length, estimate using hand-spans and verify with a scale, and use a broken scale to measure by counting from a non-zero starting point. Symbolic reasoning is extended through a number line fill-in and a word problem (stickers on a board requiring m-to-cm conversion). The section spans the full concrete→pictorial→symbolic path within one context.

tasks.description: Measure each drawn object using a scale; write the names in increasing order of length.
Estimate lengths (fingernail, eraser, ant, grain, rajma): equal to, more than, or less than 1 cm; verify by measuring. Write things shorter than 1 cm.
Race three toy cars down a ramp; measure the distance each travels in cm and rank them.
Find the longest and shortest route in a treasure-hunt map (walking on yellow 1 cm tiles); find the length of your route in centimetres.
Trace your hand on paper; measure its length using a scale.
Use your hand to estimate the measurement of objects; convert into centimetres; verify using a scale. Fill the table.
Help Ashwin measure using a broken scale (starting from a non-zero mark); find the two indicated lengths in cm.
Fill the blanks on a number line marked in 10 cm intervals (0–100 cm) and in metres (0–1 m); include 25 cm and 33 m.
A board is 2 m long; a decorative sticker is 20 cm long. How many stickers cover the board completely?

sections.title: Metre and Centimetres Together
sections.prose_summary: Students encounter dual representations of the same length (120 cm vs 1 m 20 cm) and understand that both are correct, establishing flexible conversion between metres and centimetres. A village-wells context provides conversion practice (completing tables: 2 m = 200 cm; _m = 400 cm, etc.) and a matching exercise pairs equivalent depths expressed in mixed and pure-cm form. A class measurement activity where students record heights in both cm and m reinforces the dual notation and builds a class data set for comparison questions.

tasks.description: Fill blanks so well depths are equal: 2 m = 200 cm; __ m = 400 cm; 6 m = __ cm; __ m = 800 cm.
Match wells with the same depth: four pairs of mixed-unit and pure-cm representations.
Measure each student's height using a measuring tape; complete a class table with height in cm and in m.
Answer questions: tallest height, shortest height, number of children more than 1 m tall, number shorter than 1 m.

sections.title: Perimeter — Fencing and Lacing
sections.prose_summary: Perimeter is introduced informally as the length of the boundary of a closed shape, motivated by Bhola fencing a vegetable garden. Students count boundary bricks, compare boundaries visually, and then use 1 cm dot grids to measure the perimeter of polygons precisely. Tasks move from identifying and comparing perimeters (longest/shortest/same) to measuring real classroom objects and creatively drawing shapes with a given perimeter. The section sits at the pictorial-to-symbolic level: the dot grid provides the concrete scaffold and formal cm measurement completes the journey.

tasks.description: Colour the bricks on Bhola's garden boundary; count how many bricks are needed to make the boundary.
Bhola made boundaries in three ways; circle the boundary that is longest.
Find perimeter of shapes on dot grid (1 cm spacing); colour longest boundary blue, shortest green; tick shapes with the same length.
Do any of the six shapes (A–F) have the same perimeter? Tick them.
Tick the garden with the minimum perimeter from four options.
Estimate then measure the perimeters of desk, blackboard, classroom floor, and two chosen objects; record estimated and actual values in cm/m.
Draw three different shapes each with a perimeter of 20 cm on a 1 cm dot grid.
